speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three, Wednesday, March 8, 1995. The survivor is Lola Orzech. We're in Los Angeles. I'm Carol Stahlberg. Mrs. Orzech, you were talking about the pogroms in Poland after the war, in Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? They were beating up Jews, the Poles. I never saw one, but what I heard from my brother came-- he was going to town to-- very often. And-- and my husband was present while this happened. He can tell you a little bit more. But when those things start to happen, my husband says, we have to get out of here. He was traveling. He was doing some black market-- what could he do-- black market business going back and forth to-- to Germany, or-- anyhow, he says, we are getting married. And after we got married, he says, we have to leave Poland. This is no-- no future. We want to make a-- have a family. And I even-- I wasn't too happy about it. Well, I start to cry, because my sister was still there, and my brother was there. And so that's the first time he got really mad at me. And he says, if you're not going, I'm going by myself. [LAUGHS] But of course, we went. And we went through the border. We got smuggled in through the-- that was Russian border at that time, yeah-- border, and we went to Vienna, from Vienna to Linz. And-- and there was the-- the-- Bindermichl. That was a camp for all those survivors. That's where--

speaker: interviewer
text: Displaced persons camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: --displaced person camp. And then he-- like I told you, he was making-- he took a trip from-- to Poland back and with some merchandise. I don't know whether it was leather or whatever. And he got stuck in Regensburg. And he was-- he's a very-- he was a very good soccer player, and he-- he played once there, and they didn't let him go. And they said I have to go back to Linz. My wife is there. We will bring your wife here and one here, to Regensburg. And I remember Friday night an American-- he wasn't a soldier, he was higher than that. A Jewish guy came in with a letter from my husband to pack what we had at that time-- just a suitcase with-- [LAUGHS] with-- with clothes. And I remember in Bindermichl, I had a table there in the room. So I put in a-- a paper on the table, because I didn't had a tablecloth. [LAUGHS] Anyhow, with a letter from him to-- to get this-- whatever, put in a suitcase and come. They was take me in through the border. I didn't know what to do. His brother was at that time also in Bindermichl. So I-- and my cousin-- so I sent quick my cousin-- it was Friday-- to ask his brother what to do. His brother was in the mikvah. [LAUGHS] Because for-- it was Friday night. And then when he came out, that he-- they were waiting. And so he says, you go and get your stuff. We had some other things with him. He says don't worry about it. That will be sent over. They used to call him [NON-ENGLISH]. He will come. Then he will get it. And they smuggled me through in the car through the border. I remember in Germany I was wearing always a hat and gloves. And, I don't know. I put on my hat and I got dressed. And-- and when we were passing through the border, he said-- one of them said to me, take off your hat. And you don't say another word. Let us do the talking. And they got me through the border to Germany. And then they gave me-- us their apartment. And we started our life. I got pregnant, which I wasn't-- at that time-- too happy about it, because I knew we want to travel. We want to go to United States. And I was afraid to travel with a small child, but it happened anyhow. My oldest one was born in 1949 in January. In 1949, December 17, we came to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your daughter was born right after you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was born when? When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In January 21st.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came to the United States when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a year-- in December.

speaker: interviewer
text: The following December?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh. And what's your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marti. Right now her name is Spector.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you came to the United States, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- first they sent us to Omaha-- Omaha, Nebraska. And at that time, my sister was in Canada. Then my brother-in-law had a brother here in California, in Ventura County, Santa Paula. So she came. And he made papers for them. And she came, and she was living here in California.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The one which had the baby. And when we came to Omaha, a Jewish family came to this, you know, station. They gave us-- the Jewish community center-- they gave us a-- a apartment with old furnitures. But I remember the bed was red. And it had a big hole-- the mattress had a big hole which I had to stuff with-- [LAUGHTER] --with-- with the-- you know. They paid for us for three months of-- of the rent. And well, I came with a little child-- with a little girl on my hands. I couldn't go to work. But then they found-- they found him a job. And whatever little he was making, we start to every week put away a few dollars for the rainy-- rainy days. We didn't come. We-- we came with a little-- we came with a whole big box of-- of things, instead of to bring money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we spent-- I made clothes for me, and for him, and for the baby, and-- and silverware, and-- and-- and bedding. And everything was not really necessary. It would be much smarter if we would-- if we would bring just the money what we spent on-- on that. So they gave him a job. He got a good job at-- it was a [NON-ENGLISH] Company-- Jewish people-- they own the-- he was-- they were making like the-- for the chickens, the incubator. They were giving the-- the shots. And he-- he-- they liked him there. He started to make-- nobody had overtime. They gave him overtime. And after a couple years-- that was in 1949-- in '50-- in '52, we went for vacation here to California to see my sister. And in Omaha, the weather was horrible-- summertime. And well, the winter I didn't mind, because we-- in Poland, it was the same. But summertime was horrible. And I saw the weather here in California. And my brother-in-law says, how many are we left? Why shouldn't we be together?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I never went back to Omaha-- just Stan. And he gave two weeks notice, because they were too nice people just to-- to leave like that-- like that. And he shipped whatever it was really worth it to be shipped. And we lived in Santa Paula. And over there it was, again, not so easy, because he had to start from scratch to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing all this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a little baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were just a housewife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Housewife and, you know, no language. And I was the type-- unless I knew that I'm saying OK, I didn't open up my mouth. My husband came [LAUGHS] to Omaha, and he started to talk. He was mixing the-- the German with the-- people understood him. And then, in 1954, we lived in Santa Paula. Then he wind out working for a Safeway. He was a manager. And in '54, my second daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dorothy Linda. Yeah. And then, all of a sudden, she was one year old. One Sunday comes in-- my cousin came. They found me. Yeah. And they say, what are you sitting here in a small town? Let's move to Los Angeles. And at that time, I wasn't too happy with my husband's-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --job. And-- and so we moved into-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: --we moved into LA.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of a sign?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right. So you were saying your cousins came, and you all decided to move to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They start to talk us into what you doing here in that small town-- no Jewish people? And we decided to-- he quit the job. And we decided to come to Los Angeles. There is, again, a story that he had to look, but it was easier, because he worked already for Safeway in Santa Paula. So he found a job in-- at Safeway. And he worked 10 years for Safeway. And then we went into business-- liquor stores. And by the time we had three liquor stores. And at that time, I was helping already. The girls were bigger. And that's about it-- made some investment, and we're retired now.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Let me ask you. Now that it's 50 years after the war, do you have any message that you'd like to give your children or your grandchildren about your life and what you went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, to be aware, you know, what-- what's going on-- not to let it happen, you know, again, the same-- what had-- what happened before. And the messages I give them here is a completely different-- different life-- with saving. They don't listen to me anyhow. [LAUGHS] But thank God they doing OK. My oldest daughter's son is a lawyer. And my second daughter's son, he is also a lawyer, but he's not practicing law. He was in jewelery business. Now he has a nice position working. And she's-- she took off at the jewelry, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have five grandchildren, which I am proud of. I have a granddaughter, 21, and two grandsons-- what they going to be? One is going be in April, 16. The other one, December, is going to be 16. They already drive a car. I mean, they don't drive, but they already have the permits. And my-- did I told you about my granddaughter, 21? Yeah, she's going to college. She wants to be a lawyer. She's graduating from college this year. And the two youngest one, which they were bar mitzvahed last year, they were going on 14. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we enjoy our retirement. We travel a lot-- [LAUGHS] --and play cards, and go belong to the-- to the organizations, and go for dances. And-- and we try to make the-- the, you know, the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you have some pictures that you're going to share with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us who's in this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture is my-- my grandfather. His name was David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meltzer. And then it was my grandmother, [NON-ENGLISH] Meltzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sitting, in the white blouse. Then next to her is my Aunt [NON-ENGLISH]. And to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --right is my Uncle Max. He was in-- can I comment?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was in-- he left in 1932 to Palestine. And he died-- he was 85. A few years ago, he died. He was in kibbutz. And then there was my Uncle [NON-ENGLISH], which I think he got killed in underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first row from the right is my mother. Her name is Matilda. Then it's my father, Leon. Then it's my oldest sister, Regina, then my brother Herman. And then it's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The second row, next to my brother, and then the youngest one, Marilka, and then another sister, Dora.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken outskirts of Krakow, Poland, on vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken maybe 1937, in Zakopane, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my father, my father Leon, skiing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding picture in 1940-- May 19, 1946-- my husband Stanley and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, Lola Orzech. And that's my husband, Stanley. And this is taken in Germany, Regensburg, in '40's-- 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, Lola Orzech, with my firstborn daughter, Matilda. And she was, at that time, one year old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in 19?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the, oh-- Regensburg, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany, Regensburg, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is my whole family. My name is Lola Orzech. Far left and right are my two grandsons, Jeffrey and Jason Spector.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they're twins.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they are twins. I said that they are twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but we didn't hear it on the camera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to the left is Mitchell, their brother. He's going to be now-- in December, he's going to be 16 years old. Then it's my-- his father, Bruce Spector, my son-in-law. Then it's my younger daughter, Dorothy Spector. There is my son-in-law, Steve Spector. Then it's my granddaughter, Robin Spector. There is Mark, my grandson, Mark Spector. And that's my oldest daughter, Marti Spector. And then next to her it's-- it's me, Lola Orzech, and my husband, Stanley Orzech.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken three years ago at Mark Spector's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1992.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Los Angeles, yeah. That's my-- my daughter's home-- outside of my daughter's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why does each daughter have the last name of Spector? Both your son-in-laws are named Spector?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- both son-in-laws are cousins. Their fathers were brothers. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, cool.
